---
title: "Introduction to Particle Physics I: Particles, Fields, and Interactions"
subtitle: "Baikal School · 75 min"
author: "Dmitry V. Naumov"
institute: "Joint Institute for Nuclear Research"
date: "June 2026"
---

# Orientation

## Lecture Map

::: {.timing}
::: {.time}
0-7 min
:::
::: {}
Why the particle-physics language is needed in neutrino experiments.
:::
::: {.time}
7-20 min
:::
::: {}
A particle as a field quantum and a set of quantum numbers.
:::
::: {.time}
20-32 min
:::
::: {}
Fermions, bosons, leptons, quarks, gauge bosons, and the Higgs.
:::
::: {.time}
32-47 min
:::
::: {}
Interactions, vertices, amplitudes, and Feynman diagrams.
:::
::: {.time}
47-60 min
:::
::: {}
Cross sections, decay widths, and detector event rates.
:::
::: {.time}
60-75 min
:::
::: {}
Natural units, energy scales, and checkpoint questions.
:::
:::

## Main Message

::: {.takeaway}
A particle is not a tiny ball. It is a quantum of a field with definite quantum numbers.
:::

An interaction is an allowed transition between quantum states.

It is constrained by:

::: {.compact}
- symmetries;
- conservation laws;
- kinematics;
- the existence of a nonzero interaction vertex.
:::

# Why Particle Physics

## From Matter to Quarks and Leptons

::: {.flow}
::: {}
matter
:::
::: {}
atoms
:::
::: {}
nuclei + electrons
:::
::: {}
nucleons
:::
::: {}
quarks + leptons
:::
:::

This chain is useful, but incomplete: particle physics is not only another layer in a nested-doll picture.

At this scale the language changes:

$$
\text{states},\quad
\text{fields},\quad
\text{amplitudes},\quad
\text{symmetries},\quad
\text{probabilities}.
$$

## More Than Composition

Particle physics does not only ask "what is this object made of?"

It gives a language for asking:

::: {.compact}
- which states can exist;
- which transitions between states are allowed;
- how probable a process is;
- what an experiment actually measures;
- which theory parameters enter observables.
:::

::: {.question}
For neutrinos this is essential: the incoming particle is almost never observed directly.
:::

## What a Neutrino Telescope Sees

::: {.flow}
::: {}
neutrino flux
:::
::: {}
rare interaction
:::
::: {}
charged particle or cascade
:::
::: {}
Cherenkov light
:::
::: {}
reconstructed event
:::
:::

A neutrino telescope does not see the neutrino directly.

It records secondary charged particles, cascades, and Cherenkov light.

That is why we need to understand how flux becomes events.

## Flux Is Not the Event Count

Even if the flux \(\Phi\) is known, an experiment still needs:

::: {.compact}
- the interaction probability;
- the number of targets;
- the exposure time;
- the detection efficiency;
- the reconstruction response function.
:::

::: {.takeaway}
A detector event is the end of a chain, not a direct image of the incoming neutrino.
:::

# Particle

## Particle as a Quantum State

In the modern language a particle is a state with well-defined quantum numbers.

For a free particle one usually specifies:

$$
m,\quad s,\quad Q,\quad \tau,\quad
\text{internal quantum numbers}.
$$

In QFT this state is interpreted as a quantum of the corresponding field.

::: {.muted}
"Elementary" means that no internal structure is resolved within the theory and energy range under discussion.
:::

## Quantum Numbers

A minimal list of descriptors:

::: {.compact}
- \(m\): mass;
- \(s\): spin;
- \(Q\): electric charge;
- color charge, if the particle participates in the strong interaction;
- weak quantum numbers;
- \(\tau\): lifetime, if the state is unstable;
- the rule relating the particle to its antiparticle.
:::

Quantum numbers are not just table labels. They constrain possible processes.

## Example: Electron

$$
e^-:
\quad
m_e = 0.511\ \mathrm{MeV},
\quad
s=\frac12,
\quad
Q=-1.
$$

The electron:

::: {.compact}
- is a lepton;
- carries no color;
- participates in electromagnetic and weak interactions;
- is stable in ordinary matter because there is no lighter state with the same electric charge.
:::

## Example: Positron

$$
e^+:
\quad
m_{e^+}=m_e,
\quad
s=\frac12,
\quad
Q=+1.
$$

The positron is the electron antiparticle.

::: {.takeaway}
An antiparticle is not a particle with negative mass. It is a field excitation with opposite additive charges.
:::

## Antiparticles

For a particle and its antiparticle:

::: {.compact}
- masses are equal;
- spins are equal;
- additive charges change sign;
- electric charge changes sign;
- color changes into anticolor.
:::

Examples:

$$
e^-\leftrightarrow e^+,\qquad
p\leftrightarrow \bar p,\qquad
\nu_\ell\leftrightarrow \bar\nu_\ell.
$$

## Spin

Spin is an intrinsic angular momentum of a quantum state.

It is not the rotation of a tiny rigid object.

For classification the important distinction is integer versus half-integer spin:

$$
s=\frac12,\frac32,\ldots
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\text{fermions},
$$

$$
s=0,1,2,\ldots
\quad\Rightarrow\quad
\text{bosons}.
$$

# Fermions and Bosons

## Fermions

Fermions have half-integer spin and obey Fermi-Dirac statistics.

The fundamental Standard Model fermions are:

::: {.compact}
- leptons: \(e,\mu,\tau,\nu_e,\nu_\mu,\nu_\tau\);
- quarks: \(u,d,s,c,b,t\).
:::

Fermions obey the Pauli exclusion principle.

This matters not only for atoms, but also for dense matter, decays, and phase space.

## Bosons

Bosons have integer spin and obey Bose-Einstein statistics.

In the Standard Model:

::: {.compact}
- gauge bosons: \(\gamma, g, W^\pm, Z\);
- Higgs boson: \(H\).
:::

Composite particles can also be bosons:

$$
\pi,\ K,\ \rho,\ \text{nuclei with integer total spin}.
$$

## A Useful but Dangerous Phrase

One often says:

::: {.question}
"Fermions are matter; bosons are forces."
:::

This is a useful first orientation, but not a law.

::: {.compact}
- Mesons are bosons but are composite particles.
- The Higgs is a boson, but not a "force" in the elementary-school sense.
- Gauge bosons encode interactions, but a diagram is not a mechanical picture.
:::

## Particle Classes

| Class | Spin | Examples | Comment |
|---|---:|---|---|
| Leptons | \(1/2\) | \(e,\mu,\tau,\nu_e,\nu_\mu,\nu_\tau\) | no color |
| Quarks | \(1/2\) | \(u,d,s,c,b,t\) | color |
| Gauge bosons | \(1\) | \(\gamma,g,W,Z\) | gauge interactions |
| Higgs boson | \(0\) | \(H\) | electroweak symmetry breaking |

## Leptons

Leptons do not carry color charge.

Charged leptons:

$$
e^-,\quad \mu^-,\quad \tau^-.
$$

Neutrinos:

$$
\nu_e,\quad \nu_\mu,\quad \nu_\tau.
$$

Neutrinos have \(Q=0\), so they do not participate in electromagnetic interactions.

Their Standard Model access to detector physics is through the weak interaction.

## Quarks

Quarks carry color charge and participate in the strong interaction.

Electric charges:

$$
Q_u=Q_c=Q_t=+\frac23,\qquad
Q_d=Q_s=Q_b=-\frac13.
$$

Because of confinement, free quarks are not observed as isolated particles.

Observable hadrons:

::: {.compact}
- baryons: \(qqq\), for example \(p,n\);
- mesons: \(q\bar q\), for example \(\pi,K\).
:::

## Gauge Bosons

Gauge bosons are associated with gauge interactions:

| Boson | Interaction | Couples to |
|---|---|---|
| \(\gamma\) | electromagnetic | electric charge |
| \(g\) | strong | color |
| \(W^\pm\) | weak charged current | weak doublets |
| \(Z\) | weak neutral current | electroweak charge |

In the weak interaction, neutrinos play a central role because they have no strong or electromagnetic channel.

## Higgs Boson

The Higgs boson is a scalar:

$$
s=0.
$$

In this introductory sense it is not a "fifth force." It is tied to electroweak symmetry breaking.

After symmetry breaking:

::: {.compact}
- \(W^\pm\) and \(Z\) become massive;
- the photon remains massless;
- fermion masses are parameterized by Yukawa couplings.
:::

# Interactions

## Interaction as Transition Amplitude

In QFT a process is described by a transition amplitude:

$$
i\to f,
\qquad
\mathcal M_{i\to f}.
$$

The probability is related not to the amplitude itself, but to its squared magnitude:

$$
\text{rate or cross section}
\propto
|\mathcal M|^2
\times
\text{phase space}.
$$

## What a Feynman Diagram Is

A Feynman diagram is bookkeeping for amplitudes.

It shows:

::: {.compact}
- external states of the process;
- internal propagators;
- allowed interaction vertices;
- the order in coupling constants.
:::

::: {.takeaway}
A Feynman diagram is not a photograph and not a literal particle trajectory.
:::

## Example: \(e^+e^-\to\mu^+\mu^-\)

One simple amplitude is:

$$
e^+e^-
\to
\gamma^\ast/Z^\ast
\to
\mu^+\mu^-.
$$

What is checked:

::: {.compact}
- charge conservation;
- energy-momentum conservation;
- the existence of \(e e \gamma\) and \(\mu\mu\gamma\) vertices;
- enough energy to produce \(\mu^+\mu^-\).
:::

## Example: Beta-Decay Language

At the hadron level:

$$
n\to p+e^-+\bar\nu_e.
$$

At the quark level:

$$
d\to u+W^-,
\qquad
W^-\to e^-+\bar\nu_e.
$$

This is a weak charged-current process: electric charge is carried by \(W^\pm\).

## Inverse Beta Decay

An important detector channel:

$$
\bar\nu_e+p\to n+e^+.
$$

Here the neutrino flavor is tied to the charged lepton in the final state.

The process is useful because the \(e^+\) gives a prompt signal, while neutron capture can give a delayed tag.

::: {.muted}
The details depend on medium and energy; the important point here is the process structure.
:::

## Neutrino-Nucleon Scattering

At high energies one often writes:

$$
\nu_\mu+N\to\mu^-+X.
$$

Here:

::: {.compact}
- \(N\) is a nucleon or nucleus in the target;
- \(X\) is the hadronic final state;
- \(\mu^-\) carries information about the charged-current \(\nu_\mu\) interaction.
:::

A real analysis needs parton distributions, nuclear effects, and detector response.

## Allowed Process

::: {.takeaway}
Allowed process \(\ne\) energy conservation only.
:::

A physically allowed process requires:

$$
\text{kinematics}
+\text{conservation laws}
+\text{quantum numbers}
+\text{nonzero vertex}.
$$

If one element is absent, the process is forbidden or strongly suppressed.

## Conservation Laws

In ordinary Standard Model processes one checks:

::: {.compact}
- energy-momentum conservation;
- electric charge conservation;
- color conservation;
- baryon and lepton number in ordinary perturbative SM processes;
- angular momentum;
- flavor rules specified by the interaction.
:::

Not every process that looks possible is physically available.

## Kinematic Thresholds

Even if an interaction vertex exists, a process may not occur because of a threshold.

Example:

$$
\bar\nu_e+p\to n+e^+.
$$

Enough energy is required to create the heavier final-state particles.

In detector analysis the threshold determines which parts of the spectrum are accessible.

## Example: \(\mu^-\to e^-+\gamma\)

Energetically the process is possible:

$$
m_\mu > m_e.
$$

But this does not mean that it occurs with an observable probability.

In the minimal SM without neutrino masses and flavor mixing there is no ordinary tree-level \(\mu^-\to e^-\gamma\) process.

With neutrino masses it appears only at an extremely suppressed loop level.

::: {.takeaway}
"Energy allows it" does not mean "the process happens."
:::

# Cross Sections, Decays, Events

## Decay Width and Lifetime

For an unstable particle:

$$
P(t)=e^{-t/\tau},
\qquad
\Gamma=\frac{1}{\tau}.
$$

In units with \(\hbar=c=1\), lifetime and width have inverse dimensions.

Large \(\Gamma\) means a short lifetime.

Small \(\Gamma\) means a long-lived state.

## Cross Section

The cross section \(\sigma\) measures the effective probability of a scattering process.

Dimension:

$$
[\sigma]=\text{area}.
$$

Intuitively:

::: {.compact}
- larger \(\sigma\) means a more probable process;
- smaller \(\sigma\) requires a larger target or longer exposure;
- \(\sigma\) depends on energy and process channel.
:::

## Simple Event-Rate Formula

For a counting experiment:

$$
N_{\rm events}
=
\Phi\,
\sigma\,
N_{\rm target}\,
T\,
\epsilon.
$$

Where:

::: {.compact}
- \(\Phi\) is the flux;
- \(\sigma\) is the cross section;
- \(N_{\rm target}\) is the number of targets;
- \(T\) is the exposure time;
- \(\epsilon\) is the detection efficiency.
:::

## Toy Code Fragment

This is a toy calculation, not a detector simulation.

```python
from particle_intro.event_rates import expected_events

N = expected_events(
    flux=1e10,
    cross_section=1e-44,
    n_targets=1e33,
    exposure_time=3.15e7,
    efficiency=0.8,
)
print(N)
```

The purpose of the code is to check scale, not to replace an analysis.

## Differential Event Rate

A real detector analysis is closer to:

$$
\frac{dN}{dE_{\rm rec}}
=
T
\int dE_\nu\,
\Phi(E_\nu)
\sigma(E_\nu)
R(E_{\rm rec},E_\nu)
\epsilon(E_{\rm rec}).
$$

Here \(R(E_{\rm rec},E_\nu)\) is the response function.

## Detector Response

A detector does not measure the "true neutrino energy." It measures reconstructed quantities.

This introduces:

::: {.compact}
- energy resolution;
- angular resolution;
- flavor or topology classification;
- selection efficiency;
- backgrounds;
- migration between bins.
:::

::: {.takeaway}
The physics model and detector model meet in the event prediction.
:::

## Baikal-Style Event Chain

::: {.flow}
::: {}
\(\nu\)
:::
::: {}
weak interaction
:::
::: {}
track or cascade
:::
::: {}
Cherenkov photons
:::
::: {}
event sample
:::
:::

For Baikal it is especially important to distinguish:

::: {.compact}
- long tracks from \(\nu_\mu\) charged-current events;
- cascade-like events from \(\nu_e\), \(\nu_\tau\), and neutral-current channels;
- reconstruction and selection effects.
:::

# Units and Scales

## Natural Units

In particle physics one usually uses:

$$
\hbar=c=1.
$$

Then:

::: {.compact}
- mass, energy, and momentum are measured in eV;
- length and time are inverse to energy;
- formulas become shorter, but dimensions still matter.
:::

A useful conversion:

$$
\hbar c \simeq 197.3\ \mathrm{MeV\,fm}.
$$

## Energy Scales

| Scale | Typical domain |
|---:|---|
| eV | atomic physics |
| keV | X-rays, low-energy processes |
| MeV | nuclear physics, solar neutrinos |
| GeV | hadrons, atmospheric neutrinos |
| TeV | LHC, high-energy astroparticle physics |

High energy is not the only way to search for new physics.

In neutrino physics, long baselines, small masses, rare processes, and huge exposures are also essential.

# Checkpoint

## What Should Remain

After this lecture you should be able to answer:

::: {.compact}
- what a particle is in the modern language;
- how an electron differs from a photon, a neutrino, and a quark;
- what it means for a process to be allowed;
- why cross sections and decay widths are observables;
- why a detector measures events rather than the neutrino flux itself.
:::

## Transition to Lecture 2

We now have the language:

$$
\text{particles}
\to
\text{quantum numbers}
\to
\text{interactions}
\to
\text{cross sections and decays}.
$$

In the second lecture we assemble this into the Standard Model and see why neutrinos immediately point beyond its minimal version.

## Further Reading

::: {.small}
- Griffiths, *Introduction to Elementary Particles*.
- Peskin and Schroeder, *An Introduction to Quantum Field Theory*.
- Particle Data Group, *Review of Particle Physics*.
- Formaggio and Zeller, *From eV to EeV: Neutrino Cross Sections Across Energy Scales*.
:::

::: {.muted}
Bibliographic entries in `references.bib` are intentionally marked `TODO: verify` before final publication.
:::
